events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old male named Raymond Okafor. He is of Nigerian-American descent, works as a high school principal, is a former smoker (quit 12 years ago, 15 pack-year history), drinks alcohol socially, and has a past medical history significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus (well-controlled on metformin), hypertension (on lisinopril at baseline), and hyperlipidemia. He has no family history of colorectal cancer. BMI is 28.4 kg/m2. ECOG performance status 1 at time of diagnosis.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition) ADENOCARCINOMA OF THE SIGMOID COLON (ICD-O-3 site code C18.7, histology code 8140/3). TNM staging: pT3 N2a M1a (hepatic metastases, synchronous). Summary stage: Distant. Primary tumor located in the sigmoid colon, 4.8 cm in greatest dimension, with extension through the muscularis propria into the pericolorectal tissues. Regional lymph nodes: 3 of 18 nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma. Distant metastases: multiple bilobar hepatic lesions (largest 3.2 cm in segment VII, additional lesions in segments II, IVa, VI). No peritoneal carcinomatosis identified on preoperative imaging. Microsatellite stable (MSS) by IHC. KRAS G12D mutation detected. NRAS wild-type. BRAF V600E wild-type. HER2 non-amplified. CEA at diagnosis: 187.3 ng/mL. CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 412 U/mL. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY (colonoscopic biopsy and hepatic core biopsy).

At age 58 years, the patient had a colonoscopic biopsy of the sigmoid colon mass. Pathology result: ADENOCARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED (grade 2). Immunohistochemistry: MLH1 intact, MSH2 intact, MSH6 intact, PMS2 intact (consistent with microsatellite stable tumor). KRAS mutation testing by PCR: KRAS codon 12 mutation, G12D (c.35G>A). NRAS exons 2-4: wild-type. BRAF V600E by allele-specific PCR: wild-type. Concurrent core needle biopsy of hepatic segment VII lesion confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with colorectal primary (CK20+, CDX2+, CK7-).

At age 58 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (staging CT). The study showed a 4.8 cm mass in the sigmoid colon with associated pericolic fat stranding and multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes (largest 1.9 cm). In the liver, multiple hypodense lesions were identified in both lobes: segment II (1.4 cm), segment IVa (2.1 cm), segment VI (1.7 cm), and segment VII (3.2 cm, dominant lesion). No ascites. No peritoneal implants identified. Lungs: no pulmonary nodules or masses. No bone lesions identified. Impression: T3 N2 M1a sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. No pulmonary or peritoneal disease. Cancer status: cancer present, metastatic.

At age 58 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed on the hepatic core biopsy specimen (colorectal adenocarcinoma, metastatic site). Comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) panel covering 324 genes showed: KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A, VAF 42%, pathogenic, activating mutation, direct therapeutic target), TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T, VAF 51%, pathogenic, loss-of-function), APC p.R1450* (c.4348C>T, VAF 48%, pathogenic, loss-of-function), SMAD4 p.R361H (c.1082G>A, VAF 19%, pathogenic, loss-of-function - associated with poor prognosis), PIK3CA p.E545K (c.1633G>A, VAF 18%, pathogenic, activating - potential co-driver), FBXW7 p.R465H (c.1394G>A, VAF 22%, likely pathogenic), CDK8 amplification (copy number gain, 4 copies), MYC amplification (copy number gain, 6 copies), PTEN loss (homozygous deletion, exons 1-9). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 8 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status by NGS: MSS. HER2: not amplified (CN 2). NRAS exons 2-4: wild-type. BRAF V600E: wild-type. RAS/RAF pathway: KRAS G12D activating. PI3K/AKT pathway: PIK3CA E545K activating + PTEN loss (dual pathway activation). No ERBB2 amplification. No ALK, RET, or NTRK fusions detected. No actionable fusions. Germline findings: no pathogenic variants in APC, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, MUTYH, BRCA1, BRCA2 detected (reflex germline testing completed).

At age 58 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) assessment. Medical oncology, surgical oncology, interventional radiology, and hepatobiliary surgery participated. Discussion: Primary sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases. Genomic profile notable for KRAS G12D mutation rendering anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) contraindicated. MSS status precludes immunotherapy. SMAD4 loss noted as a prognostic marker. Tumor board decision: initiation of systemic chemotherapy with FOLFOX plus bevacizumab as first-line therapy. Surgical resection of primary and liver-directed therapy (potential hepatic resection or ablation) to be reassessed after 6-8 cycles of induction chemotherapy. Patient counseled regarding goals of therapy (palliative intent with potential conversion to resectable disease). Patient expressed strong desire for aggressive treatment. Port placement recommended prior to chemotherapy initiation. Cancer present, no prior treatment.

At age 58 years, the patient underwent surgical placement of a subcutaneous implanted venous access port (right chest wall). Procedure: totally implantable venous access device (PORT-A-CATH) placement via right subclavian vein under fluoroscopic guidance. No intraoperative or immediate postoperative complications. Port functional, tip confirmed in the lower superior vena cava/cavoatrial junction by chest X-ray. The patient was also noted to have a 3 cm sigmoid polyp adjacent to the primary tumor; this was managed non-operatively given systemic therapy plan.

At age 58 years, the patient initiated first-line palliative-intent systemic chemotherapy with FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1, 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV day 1; repeated every 14 days). Baseline CEA: 187.3 ng/mL, CA 19-9: 412 U/mL. Pre-treatment ECOG PS 1. Doses calculated based on BSA 2.01 m2. Antiemetic prophylaxis with ondansetron and dexamethasone. G-CSF not routinely administered. Patient was counseled on peripheral neuropathy risk given pre-existing mild diabetic neuropathy (baseline grade 1 sensory neuropathy in bilateral feet).

At age 58 years, after cycle 3 of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (numbness and tingling in bilateral hands and feet, interfering with fine motor tasks but not with activities of daily living). Attributed to cumulative oxaliplatin toxicity, exacerbated by underlying diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Management: dose reduction of oxaliplatin to 75 mg/m2 (12% reduction per institutional guidelines). Additionally, the patient reported hypertension with blood pressure readings of 162/98 mmHg at home. This was attributed to bevacizumab-related hypertension superimposed on pre-existing hypertension. Antihypertensive regimen intensified: lisinopril dose increased from 10 mg to 20 mg daily, and amlodipine 5 mg daily added. Blood pressure subsequently improved to goal of less than 140/90 mmHg. Nausea was grade 1 and managed with prochlorperazine as needed.

At age 58 years (approximately 2 months into treatment), the patient had an oncology visit after cycle 3 of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab. Physical examination: ECOG PS 1, no palpable abdominal mass. No clinical signs of bowel obstruction. CEA trending down from 187.3 to 94.1 ng/mL. CA 19-9 down from 412 to 198 U/mL. Blood pressure 142/88 mmHg. Peripheral neuropathy grade 2 (bilateral hands and feet). Oxaliplatin dose reduced to 75 mg/m2. Blood pressure management optimized. Tolerating therapy with moderate fatigue. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 58 years (after cycle 6 of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, approximately 3 months into treatment), the patient had restaging CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Per RECIST 1.1 criteria: sigmoid colon primary tumor decreased from 4.8 cm to 3.1 cm (35% decrease). Hepatic lesions: segment II lesion decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm, segment IVa lesion decreased from 2.1 cm to 1.4 cm, segment VI lesion decreased from 1.7 cm to 1.1 cm, segment VII (dominant) decreased from 3.2 cm to 2.1 cm. Sum of target lesions: decreased from approximately 9.1 cm to 5.5 cm (39.6% reduction). No new lesions identified. No ascites. No pulmonary nodules. Overall response: PARTIAL RESPONSE by RECIST 1.1. Cancer status: cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 58 years (cycle 6 restaging, approximately 3 months), multidisciplinary tumor board re-evaluation. Partial response documented on CT. Hepatic surgery input: despite response, bilobar distribution of lesions and proximity of segment VII lesion to hepatic veins makes R0 resection challenging; recommend continued systemic therapy and reassessment after cycle 12. Interventional radiology: segment VI lesion may be amenable to ablation. Patient tolerating therapy reasonably well; neuropathy has stabilized at grade 2 with dose reduction. Decision: continue FOLFOX plus bevacizumab to complete 12 cycles, then reassess for hepatic-directed therapy and/or conversion to resection. Continue current blood pressure medications. ECOG PS 1. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 58 years, after cycle 8 of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, the patient developed worsening peripheral sensory neuropathy, now grade 3 (severe sensory loss in bilateral hands and feet causing significant functional impairment; difficulty with fine motor tasks, buttoning shirts, writing; balance impairment). Cumulative oxaliplatin dose at this point: approximately 510 mg/m2. Decision: oxaliplatin discontinued (oxaliplatin-stop strategy). 5-fluorouracil and bevacizumab continued (de-escalation to FOLFOX without oxaliplatin, essentially maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab). Patient referred to neurology for neuropathy management. Gabapentin 300 mg TID initiated for neuropathic pain and symptom relief. Blood pressure remained controlled on current regimen (lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 5 mg).

At age 58 years (after cycle 12 total, with last 4 cycles as 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab without oxaliplatin, approximately 6 months into treatment), the patient had restaging CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis. RECIST 1.1 assessment: sigmoid colon primary 2.8 cm (stable compared to cycle 6 scan). Hepatic lesions: segment II 0.7 cm, segment IVa 1.1 cm, segment VI 0.9 cm, segment VII 1.8 cm. Sum of target lesions: 4.5 cm. Overall response: PARTIAL RESPONSE sustained (51% reduction from baseline). No new hepatic lesions. Lungs: No pulmonary nodules or masses. No peritoneal disease. CEA: 42.8 ng/mL. CA 19-9: 89 U/mL. Cancer status: cancer present, partial response maintained.

At age 58 years (approximately 7 months after diagnosis), the patient underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis for resection of the primary sigmoid colon tumor. The procedure was performed by colorectal surgery given stability of hepatic disease and concern for potential future primary tumor complications. Intraoperative findings: 4.5 cm sigmoid colon mass, no gross peritoneal disease, moderate adhesions from prior inflammatory change. No liver metastases were resected at this procedure. Estimated blood loss 200 mL. No transfusion required. Post-operative course: uncomplicated. Discharged on post-operative day 4. Final pathology: moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, pT3 N2a (3/18 lymph nodes), distal margin clear (1.4 cm), proximal margin clear. No residual tumor-related perforation. Pathologic response: no significant treatment effect (less than 30% fibrosis, viable residual tumor).

At age 58 years (approximately 8 months after initial diagnosis, post-surgical recovery complete), the patient resumed maintenance systemic therapy with 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin plus bevacizumab (5-FU 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours every 14 days, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV day 1 every 14 days). This represented a planned maintenance strategy following completion of FOLFOX induction. Bevacizumab held for 4 weeks perioperatively and resumed after wound healing confirmed. CEA at resumption: 38.4 ng/mL. ECOG PS 1. Neuropathy: grade 2 (improved from grade 3 with oxaliplatin discontinuation and gabapentin).

At age 58 years (approximately 9 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had a routine oncology follow-up visit during maintenance therapy. Physical examination: ECOG PS 1, well-healed sigmoid colectomy incision, no palpable hepatomegaly. Blood pressure 136/84 mmHg, well-controlled. Peripheral neuropathy: improved to grade 2, predominantly in feet; hands somewhat better; can button shirts with difficulty. Gabapentin providing modest benefit. CEA 35.2 ng/mL (slight downtrend). CA 19-9 72.4 U/mL. Tolerating maintenance therapy well. Fatigue grade 1. No diarrhea, mucositis, or hand-foot syndrome. Planning for 4-month restaging CT. Cancer present, stable to responding on maintenance therapy.

At age 58 years (approximately 10 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had a restaging CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, prompted by rising CEA (48.7 ng/mL, up from nadir 35.2 ng/mL) and new onset mild dyspnea on exertion. RECIST 1.1 assessment: Previously resected sigmoid colon primary -- anastomotic site without recurrence. Hepatic lesions: segment II 0.9 cm (slight increase from 0.7 cm), segment IVa 1.3 cm (slight increase from 1.1 cm), segment VI 1.1 cm (stable), segment VII 2.2 cm (increase from 1.8 cm). Sum of target lesions: 5.5 cm (increase from 4.5 cm, 22% increase from nadir). NEW FINDINGS: bilateral pulmonary nodules identified -- right lower lobe 8 mm nodule (new), left upper lobe 6 mm nodule (new), right middle lobe 5 mm nodule (new). PET/CT recommended. Impression: Progressive disease by RECIST 1.1 (greater than 20% increase in sum of target lesions plus new pulmonary lesions). Cancer status: cancer present, PROGRESSIVE DISEASE on maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab.

At age 58 years (approximately 10.5 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had a PET/CT scan for characterization of new pulmonary nodules. Findings: FDG-avid hepatic lesions confirmed: segment II (SUVmax 4.2), segment IVa (SUVmax 5.8), segment VI (SUVmax 3.9), segment VII (SUVmax 6.1, dominant). FDG-avid bilateral pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 8 mm (SUVmax 3.4), left upper lobe 6 mm (SUVmax 2.9), right middle lobe 5 mm (SUVmax 2.1). All pulmonary nodules are metabolically active and consistent with metastatic disease given colorectal primary context. No additional sites of FDG-avid disease. No locoregional recurrence at anastomotic site. Impression: Progressive metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with hepatic progression and new FDG-avid bilateral pulmonary metastases. Cancer status: cancer present, progressive, M1b (hepatic and pulmonary metastases).

At age 58 years (10.5 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had an urgent oncology visit to discuss disease progression. Review of imaging confirmed progression with new bilateral pulmonary metastases and hepatic progression. CEA now 62.3 ng/mL (up from nadir 35.2). CA 19-9 now 148 U/mL. Patient reporting increased fatigue (grade 2), mild dyspnea on exertion (grade 1), and continued peripheral neuropathy (grade 2). ECOG PS 1. Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg on current medications. Discussion with patient regarding second-line treatment options. Given KRAS G12D mutation, anti-EGFR agents remain contraindicated. KRAS G12D-targeted therapy (sotorasib, adagrasib) not yet standard of care in colorectal cancer at this time. Options discussed: FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab (continuing anti-VEGF with cytotoxic switch) versus FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (anti-VEGFR2 antibody). Given patient's bevacizumab exposure and progression on bevacizumab-containing maintenance, decision made to switch to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab per RAISE trial data. Cancer present, progressive disease, initiating second-line therapy.

At age 58 years (approximately 11 months after initial diagnosis), the patient initiated second-line therapy with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1, 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours, ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV day 1; repeated every 14 days). UGT1A1 genotyping performed prior to irinotecan: UGT1A1*28/*1 heterozygous (intermediate metabolizer); irinotecan started at standard dose with close monitoring for toxicity. Antiemetic prophylaxis with ondansetron, dexamethasone, and aprepitant given higher emetic risk with irinotecan. Atropine 0.25 mg SQ available for cholinergic symptoms. Patient counseled on risk of diarrhea, alopecia, and neutropenia. Gabapentin continued for neuropathy. Antihypertensives continued (lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 5 mg).

At age 58 years (after cycle 2 of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab), the patient developed grade 3 diarrhea (greater than 7 stools per day above baseline, requiring IV hydration at infusion center). Loperamide initiated aggressively. Irinotecan dose held for one cycle, then reduced to 150 mg/m2 (17% reduction). Additionally, blood pressure worsened with ramucirumab: home readings 168/102 mmHg despite current antihypertensives. Antihypertensive regimen further intensified: amlodipine increased to 10 mg daily, and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily added. Blood pressure subsequently improved to 144/90 mmHg. Peripheral neuropathy remained at grade 2 (stable, no worsening on irinotecan-based regimen as expected given no oxaliplatin). Grade 1 alopecia noted. No febrile neutropenia.

At age 58 years (after cycle 4 of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, approximately 13 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had restaging CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. RECIST 1.1 assessment: Hepatic lesions: segment II 1.0 cm (stable), segment IVa 1.2 cm (slight decrease from 1.3 cm), segment VI 1.0 cm (stable), segment VII 2.0 cm (slight decrease from 2.2 cm). Sum of target lesions: 4.2 cm (decrease from 5.5 cm at progression, 24% decrease). Pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 7 mm (decrease from 8 mm), left upper lobe 5 mm (decrease from 6 mm), right middle lobe 5 mm (stable). Overall response: PARTIAL RESPONSE to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. CEA: 41.2 ng/mL (down from 62.3 ng/mL at progression). CA 19-9: 102 U/mL (down from 148 U/mL). No new lesions identified. No ascites or peritoneal disease. Cancer status: cancer present, partial response on second-line therapy.

At age 58 years (approximately 13.5 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncology visit with excellent performance status (ECOG PS 1). Partial response confirmed on restaging imaging. CEA trending down. Blood pressure now 138/88 mmHg on triple antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 10 mg + hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg). Peripheral neuropathy stable at grade 2 -- has adapted with occupational therapy, uses assistive devices for fine motor tasks. Diarrhea resolved with irinotecan dose reduction and loperamide. Mild alopecia (grade 1-2). Fatigue grade 1-2. Patient tolerating second-line regimen reasonably well. Plans to continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Next restaging imaging in 8 weeks. Neurology following for neuropathy. Cancer present, responding to second-line therapy.

At age 58 years (approximately 14 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent CT-guided core needle biopsy of the dominant hepatic segment VII lesion (3 cm) for liquid biopsy correlation and potential repeat molecular profiling to evaluate for acquired resistance mechanisms. Biopsy result: METASTATIC ADENOCARCINOMA consistent with colorectal primary (CK20+, CDX2+, CK7-, TTF-1-). Histology: moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, grade 2, similar to prior biopsy. No squamous or neuroendocrine differentiation. Immunohistochemistry for MSI/MMR: MLH1 intact, MSH2 intact, MSH6 intact, PMS2 intact (MSS confirmed, no acquired MMR deficiency). Ki-67 proliferative index: 45% (moderate-high). No significant treatment effect (viable tumor without extensive fibrosis). Concurrent liquid biopsy (circulating tumor DNA, ctDNA) sent for resistance mechanism analysis.

At age 58 years (approximately 14 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had repeat next-generation sequencing performed on the hepatic segment VII core needle biopsy specimen (repeat CGP at progression/re-biopsy). Panel covering 324 genes showed: KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A, VAF 48%, persistent, pathogenic), TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T, VAF 53%, persistent), APC p.R1450* (c.4348C>T, VAF 46%, persistent), SMAD4 p.R361H (c.1082G>A, VAF 21%, persistent), PIK3CA p.E545K (c.1633G>A, VAF 22%, persistent), FBXW7 p.R465H (c.1394G>A, VAF 20%, persistent), CDK8 amplification (copy number gain, 5 copies, slight increase), MYC amplification (copy number gain, 8 copies, increase from 6), PTEN loss (homozygous deletion, exons 1-9, persistent). NEW FINDING: ERBB2 (HER2) amplification detected (copy number 8, high-level amplification - potential acquired resistance mechanism and possible therapeutic target). RAS pathway: no secondary RAS mutations detected. RAF pathway: BRAF V600E wild-type; no RAF amplification. Concurrent ctDNA liquid biopsy (plasma): KRAS G12D detected (VAF 1.8%), TP53 R248W detected (VAF 2.1%), ERBB2 amplification detected (copy number 6 in plasma). TMB: 9 mutations/Mb (low). MSS confirmed. Actionable findings: ERBB2 high-level amplification (potential target for HER2-directed therapy in future lines). KRAS G12D: investigational KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors in clinical trials. PI3K pathway: dual PIK3CA activation and PTEN loss.

At age 58 years (approximately 15 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncology visit to discuss repeat molecular profiling results. Key new finding: acquired HER2 amplification detected on repeat biopsy and confirmed in ctDNA. Discussion: HER2 amplification may represent an acquired resistance mechanism or clonal evolution. In the context of ongoing partial response to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, the actionable finding of HER2 amplification suggests potential for future HER2-directed therapy (e.g., trastuzumab/pertuzumab or trastuzumab deruxtecan in later lines). KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors (investigational) discussed; patient enrolled in hospital tumor registry for potential clinical trial eligibility. Patient counseled: current plan is to continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab while responding; HER2-directed therapy and KRAS G12D-targeted trials to be considered at next progression. Patient is highly motivated and asking about clinical trials. Referral to early phase clinical trials team placed. Blood pressure 136/86 mmHg (well-controlled). ECOG PS 1. Cancer present, ongoing partial response.

At age 58 years (approximately 17 months after initial diagnosis, after 12 total cycles of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab), the patient had restaging CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. RECIST 1.1 assessment: Hepatic lesions: segment II 1.1 cm (stable), segment IVa 1.2 cm (stable), segment VI 0.8 cm (slight decrease), segment VII 1.9 cm (slight decrease). Sum of target lesions: 5.0 cm (stable compared to 4.2 cm at cycle 4 scan; slight increase of 19% from nadir, below the 20% progression threshold). Pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 7 mm (stable), left upper lobe 6 mm (slight increase from 5 mm), right middle lobe 5 mm (stable). No new lesions. No ascites or peritoneal disease. Overall assessment: STABLE DISEASE by RECIST 1.1. CEA: 49.8 ng/mL (mild uptrend from 41.2 ng/mL, but below progression threshold). CA 19-9: 118 U/mL. Cancer status: cancer present, stable disease on second-line therapy.

At age 58 years (approximately 17-18 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncology visit following stable disease on restaging imaging. ECOG PS 1. CEA mildly rising but stable disease by RECIST. Tolerating FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab with ongoing grade 2 neuropathy (attributed to prior oxaliplatin, not worsening on current regimen), well-controlled hypertension on triple antihypertensive therapy, and grade 1 fatigue and alopecia. Irinotecan at reduced dose (150 mg/m2) due to prior grade 3 diarrhea. Decision: continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Close monitoring with next restaging CT in 8 weeks. Early-phase clinical trials team evaluation ongoing -- patient assessed for a phase II trial of KRAS G12D-specific inhibitor plus chemotherapy backbone, pre-screening performed. Discussion about quality of life: patient working part-time in administrative role; using assistive devices for neuropathy-related limitations; overall functioning well. Peripheral neuropathy: stable at grade 2, follow-up with neurology. Blood pressure 134/84 mmHg. Cancer present, stable disease, continuing second-line therapy.

documents.text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, microsatellite stable, with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. Currently undergoing first-line systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFOX6 plus Avastin (oxali dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2 as of cycle 4 due to peripheral neuropathy). This study is requested following completion of 6 cycles of systemic therapy to assess treatment response. Comparison made to prior staging CT and prior colonoscopic/hepatic biopsy-confirmed adenocarcinoma. CEA recently reported at 94.1 ng/mL (down from 187.3 ng/mL at baseline). Patient has known pre-existing type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 iohexol intravenous contrast. Imaging obtained in the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis, and in the standard pulmonary window for the chest. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reformats reviewed. Oral contrast administered. No significant motion artifact. Scan quality: diagnostic.


FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or areas of consolidation are identified. The major and minor fissures are intact. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No pleural effusion on either side. No pneumothorax.

Cardiovascular and Mediastinum: The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The aorta is of normal caliber throughout. The main pulmonary artery is not enlarged. Mediastinal and hilar contours are within normal limits. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes by size criteria. Mild atherosclerotic calcifications of the coronary arteries are noted, consistent with the patient's cardiovascular risk profile. A right-sided subcutaneous chest wall port device is identified with the catheter tip projecting to the cavoatrial junction, unchanged in position from prior imaging, without evidence of infection or port-site complication.

Soft Tissues and Bones (Chest): No destructive osseous lesions of the visualized ribs, clavicles, sternum, or thoracic vertebrae. Soft tissues of the chest wall are unremarkable.


ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. Multiple hepatic lesions are again demonstrated, all of which are clearly diminished in size compared to the prior staging CT performed approximately three months ago.

-- Hepatic segment II lesion: A hypoattenuating lesion in segment II now measures approximately 0.9 cm in greatest axial dimension, decreased from 1.4 cm on the prior examination. The margins remain well-defined. There is no internal hemorrhage or necrosis to suggest treatment-related liquefaction.

-- Hepatic segment IVa lesion: The segment IVa lesion now measures approximately 1.4 cm in greatest axial dimension, decreased from 2.1 cm previously. It remains hypoattenuating relative to the adjacent parenchyma in the portal venous phase and shows no internal complexity.

-- Hepatic segment VI lesion: The segment VI lesion now measures approximately 1.1 cm, decreased from 1.7 cm on the baseline study. It continues to appear as a well-circumscribed hypoattenuating focus.

-- Hepatic segment VII lesion (dominant): The dominant lesion in segment VII, previously measuring 3.2 cm, now measures approximately 2.1 cm in greatest axial dimension. This lesion remains the largest of the hepatic metastases. It is hypoattenuating with slightly irregular internal attenuation, which may reflect early treatment response with heterogeneous involution. The lesion remains in proximity to the right hepatic vein; however, there is no definitive evidence of vascular encasement.

All four hepatic lesions show a reduction in size compared to the prior study. No new hepatic lesions are identified. There is no intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein, hepatic veins, and inferior vena cava are patent without evidence of thrombosis. No hepatic abscess. The gallbladder is present with a small gallstone; the gallbladder wall is not thickened and there is no pericholecystic fluid.

Sigmoid Colon Primary Tumor: A soft tissue mass is again identified in the sigmoid colon corresponding to the known primary tumor. The mass now measures approximately 3.1 cm in greatest dimension, decreased from 4.8 cm on the prior staging CT. There is residual pericolic fat stranding surrounding the sigmoid mass, slightly less prominent than previously. The mesenteric root in the sigmoid distribution demonstrates decreased size of the previously enlarged regional lymph nodes; the previously noted largest mesenteric node measured 1.9 cm and now the dominant node in this region measures approximately 1.0 cm in short axis. No new mesenteric or retroperitoneal adenopathy. The sigmoid colon is patent without evidence of obstruction; the small and large bowel are otherwise without significant dilatation. No pneumatosis or free air.

Other Abdominal Organs:

Spleen: Normal size and homogeneous attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Pancreas demonstrates normal morphology and attenuation without focal mass, ductal dilatation, or peripancreatic inflammatory change.

Kidneys/Adrenal Glands: Both kidneys are normal in size and symmetric in enhancement. No hydronephrosis bilaterally. A small 4 mm hypodense focus in the right renal cortex is too small to fully characterize but is most consistent with a small cyst; this was not clearly present on the prior examination but is likely a benign incidental finding given its appearance. No solid renal masses. Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and attenuation without evidence of metastatic involvement.

Other: No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or omental caking to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The abdominal aorta is of normal caliber. Mesenteric and retroperitoneal fat planes are preserved.


PELVIS:

Bowel: The sigmoid colon mass is described above. The remainder of the rectum and visualized small bowel loops appear normal. No pelvic free fluid.

Urinary Bladder: Normal wall thickness and contour. No filling defects.

Male Genitalia: Prostate is slightly enlarged, estimated 35 cc in volume, without discrete nodule. Seminal vesicles are symmetric. No pelvic lymphadenopathy by size criteria. Iliac chain lymph nodes are not enlarged.

Bones (Pelvis and Lumbar Spine): No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement in the visualized lumbosacral spine, sacrum, or pelvis. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine are present.


IMPRESSION:

1. Interval decrease in size of all four known hepatic metastases following six cycles of systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFOX6 plus bev, consistent with favorable treatment response. The dominant segment VII lesion has decreased from 3.2 cm to approximately 2.1 cm. No new hepatic lesions. No evidence of hepatic vascular involvement or biliary obstruction.

2. The primary sigmoid colon tumor has decreased in size from 4.8 cm to approximately 3.1 cm, with associated reduction in the caliber of the dominant regional mesenteric lymph node. These findings are consistent with treatment effect at the primary site.

3. No pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural disease. Right chest wall port in stable satisfactory position.

4. No ascites and no imaging findings to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis.

5. No osseous lesions suspicious for distant skeletal metastases.

6. Small incidental right renal cortical hypodense focus, likely a simple cyst; not definitively characterized on this examination but without features to suggest malignancy.

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION
All relevant disciplines reviewed the imaging in real-time. The following perspectives were offered:

HEPATOBILIARY SURGERY: Reviewed the updated CT images carefully. While all four lesions have decreased in size, the segment VII lesion at 2.1 cm remains in close proximity to the right hepatic vein and the junction of the right and middle hepatic veins. An R0 resection of the segment VII lesion at this time would require a challenging dissection with significant risk of positive margins or vascular injury. Additionally, the bilobar distribution of disease, involving segments II and IVa on the left lobe and segments VI and VII on the right, would likely require either a two-stage hepatectomy or a combined resection/ablation strategy to achieve complete treatment of all hepatic disease while preserving adequate hepatic parenchymal volume. Given the favorable ongoing response, the hepatobiliary surgery team strongly favors continued systemic therapy with reassessment at the 12-cycle mark. Specific concern: the SMAD4 mutation detected on molecular profiling is associated with hepatic-dominant metastatic spread and reduced likelihood of durable surgical control -- this is factored into the risk-benefit calculus.

INTERVENTIONAL RADIOLOGY: Segment VI lesion (currently 1.1 cm) is becoming more amenable to radiofrequency ablation or microwave ablation given its location and size. If it continues to decrease or stabilizes below 1.5 cm at cycle 12 reassessment, it could be treated percutaneously in a minimally invasive fashion, potentially allowing that lesion to be managed without open surgery. Segment II and IVa lesions are also potentially ablatable if they continue to respond. Segment VII remains the most technically challenging target given proximity to major vascular structures.

COLORECTAL SURGERY: The sigmoid colon primary at 3.1 cm remains unresected. No evidence of impending obstruction or perforation. Will reassess the primary at the 12-cycle mark; primary resection may be considered if hepatic disease is adequately controlled or if the patient is a potential conversion candidate.

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY: In the context of this excellent partial response with approximately 40% reduction in target lesion burden at just 6 cycles, continuing on the current FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab regimen is the most appropriate strategy. We plan to continue through cycle 12 with reassessment, then reconvene the MDT for a definitive discussion about hepatic resection, staged resection/ablation, or other hepatic-directed strategies.

CONSENSUS DECISION: Continue FOLFOX6 (oxaliplatin at reduced dose 75 mg/m2) plus bevacizumab through cycle 12. Restaging imaging to be performed after cycle 12. Full multidisciplinary reassessment for hepatic surgery and/or ablation at that time. No changes to current supportive medications.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

IMPRESSION: Mr. Okafor is a 58-year-old male with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, SMAD4 loss, PIK3CA E545K, PTEN loss) who has developed unequivocal disease progression on maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus bev. He now has hepatic progression (all four prior lesions slightly larger) plus new FDG-avid bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic spread, representing a change from M1a (hepatic-only) to M1b (hepatic plus pulmonary) disease. Rising CEA (62.3 ng/mL) and CA 19-9 (148 U/mL) are concordant with imaging findings. His ECOG PS remains 1 and he is overall tolerating his situation with remarkable resilience.

The decision-making for second-line systemic therapy in this patient is informed by several key factors:

1. KRAS G12D mutation: Anti-EGFR agents (Erbitux/Erbitux, Vectibix/Vectibix) remain absolutely contraindicated given RAS-mutant disease. KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors (such as investigational sotorasib-based combinations or adagrasib-based studies) are being evaluated in clinical trials for colorectal cancer, but none are currently approved as a standard second-line treatment specifically in this setting. The patient has been entered in our tumor registry and the early-phase trials team has been notified for potential trial enrollment as an option.

2. bev progression: He progressed on a bev-containing maintenance regimen. For second-line therapy following Avastin-based first-line treatment, the RAISE trial demonstrated a survival benefit for FOLFIRI plus ramu (8 mg/kg IV) compared to FOLFIRI plus placebo. The VELOUR data with aflibercept plus FOLFIRI is also an option. Continuing bev with a cytotoxic switch (FOLFIRI plus Avastin) is an alternative per some guidelines and the TML/ML18147 data, but given that progression occurred on Avastin-containing therapy, switching to Cyramza (a distinct anti-angiogenic targeting VEGFR2) is the preferred approach at this institution.

3. iri has not been used previously; he received FOLFOX-based therapy (Eloxatin + 5-FU) in the first line. Therefore, FOLFIRI brings a new cytotoxic mechanism (topoisomerase I inhibition) to the table.

4. MSS status precludes checkpoint immunotherapy (pembro, Opdivo) in the absence of tumor-specific qualifying mutations. TMB was low (8 mut/Mb) and he would not be expected to benefit from pembro based on MSS/TMB-low profile.

5. SMAD4 loss in the context of overall trajectory is noted as a factor associated with more aggressive biology and poorer prognosis, but does not alter current therapeutic selection.

6. UGT1A1 genotyping is being ordered today prior to Camptosar initiation. Given his known pre-existing hepatic lesions, LFTs are within normal limits, which is reassuring for iri dosing. Will await UGT1A1 result before finalizing iri starting dose.

PLAN:

1. SECOND-LINE THERAPY -- FOLFIRI PLUS Cyramza: Plan to initiate FOLFIRI plus ramu at the next available infusion slot (within 7-10 days, pending UGT1A1 results and port flushing). Planned regimen: iri 180 mg/m2 IV day 1 (dose to be confirmed pending UGT1A1 result -- will reduce to 150 mg/m2 if homozygous UGT1A1*28/*28), leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1 followed by 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours, and ramu 8 mg/kg IV day 1, every 14 days. BSA 2.01 m2 (unchanged).

2. ANTIEMETIC PROPHYLAXIS: Given higher emetogenic risk with iri versus prior 5-FU maintenance, prophylaxis will be upgraded to include aprepitant 125 mg PO day 1 / 80 mg day 2 and 3 (or fosaprepitant IV alternative on infusion day), plus ondansetron 8 mg IV/PO and dexamethasone 12 mg IV on day 1. Lorazepam 0.5-1 mg PO PRN breakthrough nausea. Atropine 0.25 mg subcutaneous available at infusion chair for acute cholinergic reaction (early-onset diarrhea, lacrimation, flushing) during or immediately after Camptosar infusion.

3. DIARRHEA MANAGEMENT: Patient counseled extensively on high-dose loperamide protocol (4 mg at first loose stool, then 2 mg every 2 hours until diarrhea-free for 12 hours; maximum 16 mg/day). Written instructions provided. iri can cause both early cholinergic diarrhea (within minutes to hours, managed with atropine) and delayed diarrhea (days 3-10, managed with aggressive loperamide). If diarrhea is grade 2 or higher and not controlled with loperamide within 24 hours, patient instructed to call oncology triage line immediately for IV hydration consideration.

4. BLOOD PRESSURE MONITORING: Blood pressure today is 138/86 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 5 mg. Cyramza is associated with hypertension (similar to Avastin), and blood pressure management will need close monitoring during ramu-based therapy. Antihypertensive intensification anticipated. Patient instructed to continue home blood pressure monitoring twice daily and report readings above 160/100 mmHg.

5. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY: Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy from prior Eloxatin is expected to be stable on FOLFIRI-based regimen (Camptosar does not cause peripheral neuropathy). Gabapentin 300 mg TID continued. Neurology follow-up maintained. No changes to neuropathy management at this time.

6. PORT ACCESS AND MAINTENANCE: Port-a-cath maintained in right chest wall. Accessed at infusion center for Avastin and 5-FU maintenance. Will continue to use for FOLFIRI plus ramu infusions.

7. DIABETES MANAGEMENT: Fasting glucose mildly elevated at 124 mg/dL today. Metformin 1000 mg BID continued. Patient to follow up with primary care physician for HbA1c recheck; the addition of dexamethasone in premedication regimen may transiently worsen glycemic control on infusion days -- patient counseled and advised to check blood sugars on infusion days.

8. ANEMIA: Hemoglobin mildly decreased at 12.1 g/dL. Likely anemia of chronic disease. No transfusion indicated. Will monitor; will check iron studies and reticulocyte count at next visit. No ESA therapy planned.

9. CLINICAL TRIALS: Referral to early-phase clinical trials team placed. Patient is a potential candidate for investigational KRAS G12D-specific inhibitor trials in the colorectal cancer setting. Tumor registry enrollment confirmed. Will coordinate with phase I/II team for screening evaluation.

10. RESTAGING IMAGING: After approximately 4 cycles (8 weeks) of FOLFIRI plus ramu, restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast will be obtained to assess early response.

11. FOLLOW-UP: Patient to return to clinic in 2 weeks (after cycle 1 day 1 of new regimen) for toxicity check and laboratory evaluation, and again at approximately 4 weeks.

The patient and his wife were present for this discussion. The situation was explained in clear terms, including the fact that this is a change in treatment intent (we remain in the palliative setting; the goal is disease control, symptom management, and prolongation of quality life). Mr. Okafor expressed understanding and continues to express a strong desire for aggressive treatment. He asked about clinical trials and was given information about the early-phase program. He will consider his options regarding trial enrollment and follow up with that team. His wife asked about long-term prognosis; an honest but compassionate discussion was held, emphasizing that disease control is achievable but cure is not our goal in this setting. Emotional support resources (social work, palliative care consultation, patient navigator) were offered; patient declined formal palliative care consultation at this time but agreed to see the oncology social worker.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Part A -- Sigmoid Colon Mass, Colonoscopic Biopsy:
Histologic sections of the sigmoid colon biopsy demonstrate fragments of colonic mucosa and submucosa infiltrated by a malignant epithelial neoplasm. The tumor is composed of irregular, angulated, and back-to-back glandular structures with marked architectural distortion. The glands are lined by columnar to cuboidal cells with moderate to abundant amphophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear features include moderate to marked pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, increased nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, and coarse chromatin. Mitotic figures are readily identified, including atypical forms. Intraluminal dirty necrosis (comedonecrosis) is present within many of the neoplastic glands, a characteristic finding of colorectal-type adenocarcinoma. A desmoplastic stromal response is present. The muscularis mucosae is disrupted, and tumor infiltrates into the submucosa in sampled fragments. There is no squamous differentiation, signet ring cell change, or mucinous component identified in these biopsy fragments (mucinous component comprising less than 50% of the tumor). Moderate acute and chronic inflammatory infiltrate is present in the adjacent stroma. Background colonic mucosa shows areas of reactive change.

Histologic grade: MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED (Grade 2) per WHO classification (irregular tubular architecture with readily identifiable gland formation comprising the majority of the tumor).

Part B -- Hepatic Segment VII Lesion, CT-Guided Core Needle Biopsy:
Histologic sections of the hepatic core biopsy demonstrate cores of tissue composed predominantly of metastatic carcinoma with a minor component of compressed, non-neoplastic hepatic parenchyma at the periphery of one core. The neoplasm is architecturally and cytologically identical to the primary sigmoid colon lesion: irregular, back-to-back malignant glands with intraluminal dirty necrosis, a desmoplastic stroma, and columnar cells with moderate nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli. No hepatocellular carcinoma pattern (trabecular, acinar) is identified. The residual hepatic parenchyma shows mild macro- and microvesicular steatosis consistent with background fatty liver change; no significant fibrosis is appreciated in the non-neoplastic liver on limited sampling. Portal tracts are not well-represented in the biopsy cores.

COMMENT:
The combined findings of Parts A and B confirm a diagnosis of moderately differentiated colorectal adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous hepatic metastasis to segment VII. The immunophenotype of the hepatic lesion (CK20+/CDX2+/CK7-) is classic for colorectal origin and excludes other primary sites in the differential including lung adenocarcinoma, pancreaticobiliary adenocarcinoma, and cholangiocarcinoma. The intact expression of all four MMR proteins by IHC is consistent with a microsatellite stable (MSS/pMMR) tumor. MSS colorectal cancers represent approximately 85% of metastatic colorectal cancers and are associated with a lower probability of response to immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy. The identified KRAS p.G12D mutation is the most common KRAS alteration in colorectal cancer (accounting for approximately 40% of all KRAS-mutant CRC) and is associated with primary resistance to anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody therapy. Comprehensive genomic profiling on the hepatic core biopsy (highest anticipated tumor cellularity) is pending and will provide additional clinically actionable information including HER2 amplification status, extended mutation profiling, and tumor mutational burden.

Results communicated to the ordering physician team. Correlation with clinical and radiologic findings recommended.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Okafor carries a diagnosis of stage IV KRAS G12D-mutant, microsatellite-stable adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases, now with pulmonary metastases identified at progression approximately 2.5 months ago. His disease history is summarized briefly below for context.

He was initially diagnosed approximately 13 months ago with a synchronous T3N2aM1a sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma. Molecular profiling at diagnosis was notable for KRAS G12D mutation, BRAF wild-type, MSS status, and a complex co-mutational landscape including TP53 R248W, APC loss, SMAD4 loss, PIK3CA E545K, and PTEN deletion. He completed 12 cycles of FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab as first-line therapy with initial good response (partial response at cycle 6), though oxaliplatin was discontinued after cycle 8 due to dose-limiting grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (cumulative oxaliplatin exposure approximately 510 mg/m2). Maintenance was continued with 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab. He underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy approximately 7 months after diagnosis for local control of the primary tumor, with an uneventful postoperative course.

Approximately 10 months after diagnosis, restaging CT showed hepatic progression (enlargement of all four hepatic lesions, with the dominant segment VII lesion increasing from 1.8 to 2.2 cm) along with new bilateral pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 8 mm, left upper lobe 6 mm, right middle lobe 5 mm). PET/CT confirmed FDG-avidity of all hepatic and pulmonary lesions, confirming progressive metastatic disease with M1b status (hepatic and pulmonary). CEA at progression was 62.3 ng/mL, up from nadir of 35.2 ng/mL. Anti-EGFR therapy remained contraindicated given KRAS G12D status; immunotherapy was not an option given MSS status.

Given progression on bevacizumab-containing maintenance therapy, second-line treatment was initiated with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (per RAISE trial data), irinotecan 180 mg/m2 day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 bolus and 2400 mg/m2 over 46-hour infusion, and ramucirumab 8 mg/kg day 1, every 14 days. UGT1A1 genotyping prior to irinotecan showed *1/*28 heterozygous status (intermediate metabolizer), and irinotecan was started at standard dose with close monitoring. He developed grade 3 diarrhea after cycle 2 requiring IV hydration at the infusion center. Irinotecan was held for one cycle and subsequently dose-reduced to 150 mg/m2 (17% reduction). Ramucirumab-related hypertension also emerged, requiring intensification of his antihypertensive regimen -- amlodipine was uptitrated from 5 mg to 10 mg daily and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg was added to his existing lisinopril 20 mg, achieving adequate blood pressure control.

He is now presenting after completing cycle 4 of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (at the reduced irinotecan dose), following restaging CT scan reviewed at today's visit, which shows a favorable response to second-line therapy.

Today, Mr. Okafor reports that he is feeling better than he was at the time of progression. His energy has improved. He denies any significant abdominal pain or cramping. He reports that diarrhea has resolved since the irinotecan dose reduction and with judicious loperamide use -- bowel movements are back to 1-2 per day, soft and formed. He notes mild nausea during the first 2-3 days of each cycle, well controlled with his antiemetic regimen (ondansetron, dexamethasone, and aprepitant). He reports mild fatigue, which he rates as manageable (grade 1-2), and he has been able to continue working part-time in an administrative capacity at the high school where he is principal. He notes grade 1-2 alopecia and is mildly self-conscious about hair thinning but tolerating this without significant distress. He reports no blood in stool, no abdominal distension, no obstruction symptoms, and no dyspnea at rest. He does still notice mild dyspnea on moderate exertion (walking briskly up stairs), though this has not worsened. He denies fever, chills, rigors, or signs of infection. He reports continued numbness and tingling in his feet bilaterally and the distal tips of his fingers from residual oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy; this has not worsened on the current irinotecan-based regimen as anticipated. He is taking gabapentin 300 mg three times daily for neuropathic discomfort. He denies falls but does continue to use a handrail when navigating stairs.

His blood pressure at home has been running in the 130s-140s systolic over the 80s-90s diastolic, which he considers improved. He has been adherent to his antihypertensive medications.

COMMENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The histologic and immunophenotypic features of this CT-guided core needle biopsy of the hepatic segment VII lesion are consistent with metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma, concordant with the previously established sigmoid colon primary (original diagnostic pathology on file). The tumor is moderately differentiated, grade 2, and morphologically similar to the original colonoscopic biopsy specimen.

There is no significant histologic evidence of treatment effect (Ryan equivalent Grade 3), consistent with the clinical scenario of ongoing viable metastatic disease in the liver during systemic chemotherapy. Intact MMR protein expression by IHC (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) confirms MSS status, with no evidence of acquired MMR deficiency -- a potentially important finding as MSI-H acquisition can occur in rare cases following chemotherapy and would have implications for immunotherapy eligibility.

The equivocal (2+) HER2 IHC result in this re-biopsy specimen is a new finding relative to the prior biopsy (which showed HER2 non-amplified status). HER2 FISH has been ordered to clarify HER2 amplification status. Correlation with comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) results (pending) will be important. Acquired HER2 amplification is a recognized resistance mechanism in KRAS-mutant colorectal cancer, particularly following anti-VEGF-based therapy, and may have potential therapeutic implications if confirmed. Clinical correlation with the treating oncologist is recommended.

No germline implications are identified from this somatic tumor specimen; germline testing was previously completed (result on file).

Results of HER2 FISH, NGS comprehensive genomic profiling, and ctDNA liquid biopsy will be issued as addenda or separate reports as results become available.

CLINICAL INDICATION: Metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma (sigmoid colon primary, KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS), previously profiled at diagnosis; repeat CGP requested at time of re-biopsy to evaluate for acquired resistance mechanisms in the context of ongoing second-line therapy (FOLFIRI plus ramu) and rising CEA. SPECIMEN INFORMATION

Primary specimen: CT-guided core needle biopsy, hepatic segment VII (dominant lesion, 3.0 cm by CT). Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue, 4 cores submitted.
Concurrent specimen: Peripheral blood, 10 mL in cell-free DNA (cfDNA) Streck tube, for liquid biopsy / circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) analysis.
Tissue adequacy: Adequate for analysis. Estimated tumor cellularity: 55% (assessed by H&E prior to nucleic acid extraction). Necrosis: minimal (<10%). Total DNA extracted: 1,340 ng. Quality metrics: DNA quality index (DQI) 0.87 (satisfactory). Library preparation: hybridization capture-based; median unique coverage depth 752x (FFPE tissue), 2,100x (cfDNA).

---

METHODOLOGY

Comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) was performed using a hybrid-capture next-generation sequencing panel encompassing 324 cancer-relevant genes, including full exonic coverage of all 324 genes, select intronic regions for fusion/rearrangement detection (e.g., ALK, RET, NTRK1/2/3, ROS1, FGFR2, ERBB2), copy number assessment by sequencing depth analysis, and microsatellite instability (MSI) determination by analysis of 114 intronic microsatellite loci. Tumor mutational burden (TMB) calculated from somatic mutation count across the sequenced genome equivalent (1.1 Mb coding sequence). Germline variants are assessed by comparison with matched normal tissue (blood-derived germline reference); germline findings are reported separately per institutional policy. Liquid biopsy (ctDNA): plasma-derived cfDNA analyzed on a 74-gene solid tumor liquid biopsy panel with digital droplet PCR (ddPCR) orthogonal confirmation for selected variants. Analytical sensitivity for ctDNA: SNVs and indels >=0.1% VAF; copy number amplifications >=1.5-fold gain above background.

---

TISSUE NGS FINDINGS

A. SOMATIC SHORT VARIANT ANALYSIS (SNVs and Indels)

1. KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A) | Exon 2 | VAF: 48% | Interpretation: PATHOGENIC -- Activating missense mutation. Classical RAS hotspot variant, substitution of glycine to aspartate at codon 12. This mutation impairs intrinsic GTPase activity, locking KRAS in the GTP-bound (active) signaling state. KRAS G12D activates downstream RAF/MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT effector pathways constitutively. This variant was detected at diagnosis (VAF 42%) and persists at comparable allele frequency (VAF 48%), consistent with retention of the founding clonal driver. Clinical significance: This mutation renders anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies (Erbitux, pani) contraindicated per current NCCN guidelines. KRAS G12D-specific covalent inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133 class) are under active clinical investigation. NOTE: Compared to baseline (VAF 42%), the modest increase in VAF (48%) is consistent with clonal dominance or increased tumor purity in the re-biopsy specimen; does not indicate new subclonal emergence.

2. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T) | Exon 7 | VAF: 53% | Interpretation: PATHOGENIC -- Loss-of-function missense mutation. Arginine-248-to-tryptophan hotspot mutation at a DNA-contact residue of the TP53 DNA-binding domain; one of the most recurrently mutated TP53 codons across human cancers. Abrogates wild-type p53 transcriptional activity and may confer gain-of-function oncogenic properties (dominant negative and gain-of-function p53 activities). Persistent at essentially identical VAF compared to baseline (VAF 51%); founding clonal event. No therapeutic implication in isolation, but indicates global genomic instability potential.

3. APC p.R1450* (c.4348C>T) | Exon 15 | VAF: 46% | Interpretation: PATHOGENIC -- Nonsense (truncating) mutation. Premature stop codon resulting in a truncated APC protein lacking the C-terminal SAMP repeats required for Axin binding and beta-catenin degradation complex assembly. This results in constitutive WNT/beta-catenin pathway activation. Classic two-hit inactivation in CRC; persistent founding driver. VAF 46% vs. 48% at baseline (essentially unchanged).

4. SMAD4 p.R361H (c.1082G>A) | Exon 8 | VAF: 21% | Interpretation: PATHOGENIC -- Loss-of-function missense mutation in the MH2 (MAD homology 2) domain. SMAD4 is the central mediator of TGF-beta/BMP signaling; loss of SMAD4 impairs anti-proliferative TGF-beta signaling. SMAD4 loss in colorectal cancer is associated with hepatic metastatic predilection and is a validated poor prognostic biomarker (specifically associated with inferior outcomes in patients with liver metastases). Persistent at VAF 21% (baseline VAF 19%). Of note, the relatively lower VAF compared to APC/TP53/KRAS is consistent with SMAD4 loss occurring in a subclonal context at diagnosis; current VAF is stable, suggesting no significant subclonal expansion.

5. PIK3CA p.E545K (c.1633G>A) | Exon 9 | VAF: 22% | Interpretation: PATHOGENIC -- Activating missense mutation in the helical domain of the p110-alpha catalytic subunit of PI3-kinase. E545K is a hotspot activating mutation that disrupts inhibitory interactions with the p85 regulatory subunit, leading to constitutive PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation. Combined with PTEN loss (see copy number below), this patient harbors dual PI3K pathway activation. Persistent at VAF 22% (baseline VAF 18%). No significant subclonal change.

6. FBXW7 p.R465H (c.1394G>A) | Exon 9 | VAF: 20% | Interpretation: LIKELY PATHOGENIC -- Missense mutation in the WD40 beta-propeller substrate recognition domain of FBXW7 (F-box and WD repeat domain-containing protein 7). FBXW7 functions as the substrate recognition component of the SCF (SKP1-CUL1-F-box protein) ubiquitin ligase complex, targeting key oncoproteins (cyclin E, MYC, JUN, NOTCH, mTOR) for proteasomal degradation. Loss of FBXW7 activity leads to accumulation of these oncoproteins and promotes cell cycle progression, angiogenesis, and metastasis. Arginine-465 is a recurrently mutated residue at the substrate-binding interface. Persistent at VAF 20% (baseline VAF 22%); stable.

B. COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS

1. ERBB2 (HER2) HIGH-LEVEL AMPLIFICATION | Chromosome 17q12 | Copy Number: 8 (high-level amplification) | Interpretation: PATHOGENIC -- NEW FINDING (not detected at baseline; baseline copy number 2, diploid). High-level ERBB2 amplification, defined as copy number >=6 relative to chromosome 17 centromere reference, is consistent with gene amplification. HER2/ERBB2 amplification in metastatic colorectal cancer occurs de novo in approximately 2-3% of cases (enriched in RAS/RAF wild-type tumors) but can also arise as an acquired resistance mechanism following anti-EGFR or anti-VEGF therapy, or through clonal evolution. In this patient, the detection of high-level HER2 amplification at re-biopsy (not present at diagnosis) is strongly consistent with ACQUIRED HER2 amplification as a clonal evolution event, potentially representing an adaptive resistance mechanism under selective pressure of anti-VEGF-based therapy. HER2 amplification in colorectal cancer is recognized as a potentially actionable target; HER2-directed therapies (trast/pertu [MOUNTAINEER trial], Tykerb/trast, and T-DXd [T-DXd/DESTINY-CRC01/02]) have demonstrated activity in HER2-amplified mCRC. Protein expression: Concurrent immunohistochemistry (IHC) for HER2 protein: 3+ staining (uniform strong membranous staining in >10% of tumor cells) -- confirming high-level protein overexpression consistent with amplification. FISH confirmation: ERBB2/CEP17 ratio 4.2 (ratio >2.0 confirms amplification by ASCO/CAP criteria). This is the most clinically actionable new finding in this repeat profiling.

2. CDK8 AMPLIFICATION | Chromosome 13q12.13 | Copy Number: 5 (moderate gain, increased from 4 at baseline) | Interpretation: Potentially relevant -- CDK8 (cyclin-dependent kinase 8) is a component of the Mediator complex and regulates transcription of genes involved in WNT/beta-catenin, NF-kappaB, and SMAD2/3 signaling. CDK8 copy number gain is recurrently observed in colorectal cancer. Modest increase from baseline (4 to 5 copies). CDK8 inhibitors (e.g., SEL120, BCD-115) are in early clinical development. Not currently standard-of-care actionable.

3. MYC AMPLIFICATION | Chromosome 8q24.21 | Copy Number: 8 (high-level gain, increased from 6 at baseline) | Interpretation: Potentially relevant -- MYC (c-Myc) is a transcription factor proto-oncogene involved in cell proliferation, growth, and metabolism. High-level MYC amplification in CRC is associated with aggressive biology and resistance to conventional cytotoxic therapy. Increase from 6 copies (baseline) to 8 copies (current) may reflect ongoing genomic instability or subclonal selection. No directly actionable MYC-targeted therapy available in standard practice. Direct MYC inhibition remains an active area of research (OMOMYC, MYC-MAX interaction inhibitors).

4. PTEN HOMOZYGOUS DELETION | Chromosome 10q23.31 | Exons 1-9 | Copy Number: 0 (homozygous deletion, complete) | Interpretation: PATHOGENIC -- Complete loss of PTEN function. PTEN is a lipid phosphatase and canonical tumor suppressor that antagonizes PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling by dephosphorylating PIP3 to PIP2. Homozygous PTEN deletion eliminates all PTEN activity, producing maximal PI3K pathway derepression. Combined with PIK3CA E545K activating mutation (above), this represents dual PI3K/AKT pathway activation. Persistent from baseline (no change in deletion extent). PTEN loss may also contribute to resistance to HER2-directed therapies (through bypass of PI3K pathway downstream of HER2); this is a potentially important co-occurring alteration in the context of the newly detected ERBB2 amplification.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

IMPRESSION:
Mr. Okafor is a 58-year-old man with metastatic MSS, KRAS G12D-mutant sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma who has had a reasonably favorable trajectory -- currently in partial response on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramu. Today's visit is focused on the important and clinically significant finding of acquired HER2 (ERBB2) amplification identified on repeat tissue biopsy and confirmed on liquid biopsy, representing clonal evolution under selective treatment pressure.

1. ACQUIRED HER2 AMPLIFICATION -- DISCUSSION AND PLAN
I spent considerable time today explaining this finding to Mr. Okafor and his wife. I framed this in both a cautionary and potentially optimistic light:

What this means: HER2 amplification in colorectal cancer, when acquired during treatment, is recognized as a potential mechanism of treatment resistance. It leads to overexpression of the HER2 receptor (the same protein targeted by Herceptin and other anti-HER2 therapies in breast and gastric cancers), which can activate downstream survival signaling pathways, partially circumventing the VEGF blockade from ramu and the cytotoxic effects of the Camptosar/5-FU backbone. Importantly, this is not present in the germline, confirming this is a somatic acquired event, not an inherited predisposition.

Current situation -- this does NOT change his immediate treatment: Critically, Mr. Okafor's disease is NOT progressing at this time. He remains in partial response. Therefore, we are NOT switching therapy today. We will continue FOLFIRI plus Cyramza while the disease is controlled, with close surveillance.

Future planning -- HER2-directed therapy: When his disease eventually progresses on FOLFIRI/Cyramza, HER2 amplification opens a meaningful therapeutic avenue. In HER2-amplified (HER2-positive) colorectal cancers, anti-HER2 combinations have demonstrated activity:
- Herceptin (Herceptin) plus Perjeta (Perjeta), per the HERACLES and MyPathway data in HER2+ mCRC
- T-DXd (Enhertu; T-DXd), which has shown impressive response rates in HER2-positive solid tumors including colorectal cancer in the DESTINY-CRC trials, with responses even in KRAS-mutant HER2-amplified CRC in later lines (though data are emerging)
- tuca-based combinations (e.g., MOUNTAINEER data with tuca plus Herceptin)

I counseled him that KRAS mutation has historically been associated with somewhat lower response rates to HER2-directed therapies in CRC compared to KRAS wild-type, but the data continue to mature. T-DXd and tuca-based combinations may have activity even in KRAS-mutant HER2-amplified tumors. This will be a key option at next progression.

KRAS G12D-directed therapy: I reiterated that sotorasib (Lumakras) and adagrasib (Krazati) have established activity in KRAS G12C-mutant cancers (lung, colorectal) but our patient has KRAS G12D, a distinct mutation. KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors (MRTX1133 and others) are in early-phase trials with promising preclinical and emerging clinical data. His referral to our early-phase clinical trials team is active; they are pre-screening him for a phase II study of a KRAS G12D inhibitor. I encouraged him to follow up with that team.

2. ACTIVE TREATMENT -- FOLFIRI PLUS Cyramza, CYCLE 8 DOSING
He will proceed with cycle 9 of FOLFIRI plus Cyramza today.
- iri: 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced, stable at this dose since cycle 3)

These modest rises do not drive a change in therapy at this point given imaging stability, but will factor into next restaging assessment. Next restaging CT is planned after 2 more cycles (in approximately 4 weeks).

3. HYPERTENSION
Blood pressure 136/86 mmHg today -- well within goal range on triple antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg). Continue current regimen unchanged. Will continue home BP monitoring with readings logged. Remind patient to call if readings consistently above 150/95 mmHg.

Discuss with neurology whether there is any additional pharmacologic or physical therapy intervention to optimize function. 7. CLINICAL TRIALS REFERRAL

Will also alert the team to the newly identified HER2 amplification in case this opens additional trial options. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Okafor is a 59-year-old male with KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV (M1b) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic and bilateral pulmonary metastases. He is approximately 17-18 months from initial diagnosis, currently on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, and showing stable disease on today's restaging review. Molecular profiling at ~14 months identified acquired HER2 amplification as a new finding, which we are tracking as a potential therapeutic target for future lines.

1. METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER -- STABLE DISEASE ON SECOND-LINE FOLFIRI PLUS RAMUCIRUMAB:
The imaging is reassuring and demonstrates that the disease is under control, albeit not shrinking further from the peak response achieved early in the FOLFIRI/ramucirumab course. I discussed the CEA trend at length with Mr. Okafor and his wife. I explained that a CEA of 49.8 -- while somewhat above the nadir of ~35 -- remains far below where we started at second-line initiation (62.3) and dramatically below diagnosis (187.3). The imaging correlates well with what we expected: stable lesions, no new disease. I reassured them that we do not make treatment decisions on CEA alone when imaging is stable and there are no new clinical symptoms of concern.

Plan: Continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab at current doses (irinotecan 150 mg/m2, ramucirumab 8 mg/kg). Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 8 weeks. I will continue to follow CEA and CA 19-9 with each cycle. If at any point the imaging demonstrates unequivocal progression (either meaningful growth of known lesions or new sites of disease), we will reassess the treatment strategy at that time.

We discussed the general trajectory: FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab has data supporting a median progression-free survival of approximately 5.7 months from the RAISE trial, and he has now been on this regimen for 6-7 months -- an encouraging sign. The plan is to continue it as long as it is controlling the disease and the toxicity profile remains acceptable.

2. ACQUIRED HER2 AMPLIFICATION -- CLINICAL TRIAL PLANNING FOR FUTURE LINES:
The repeat biopsy and liquid biopsy at 14 months confirmed high-level HER2 amplification (copy number 8 on tissue, 6 on ctDNA), in the setting of KRAS G12D persistence. This HER2 amplification may represent clonal selection under prior anti-VEGF pressure or a co-existing subclone that has expanded. In the colorectal cancer setting, HER2 amplification (seen in approximately 2-3% of CRC at baseline, higher in later lines) is an emerging actionable target. Trastuzumab plus pertuzumab and trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd, Enhertu) have shown activity in HER2-amplified/overexpressing metastatic CRC in clinical trials. The MOUNTAINEER and DESTINY-CRC01/02 trials are informative in this space. We will consider these as options at next progression.

Regarding KRAS G12D: investigational KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors are in early clinical development in CRC. I have referred Mr. Okafor to our early phase trials team. Pre-screening results are pending; the trials team will contact the family if a suitable study opens and he meets eligibility criteria. The interplay between HER2 amplification and KRAS G12D mutation may complicate single-agent KRAS inhibitor activity (as KRAS-mutant tumors with co-activating pathway alterations like PIK3CA E545K + PTEN loss may have intrinsic resistance mechanisms), and this will be weighed when considering enrollment.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY -- GRADE 2, STABLE:
Neuropathy improved from its nadir of grade 3 following oxaliplatin discontinuation approximately 9 months ago. Currently stable at grade 2. Not worsening on irinotecan (as expected -- irinotecan does not contribute to peripheral neuropathy). Gabapentin 300 mg TID being continued; I discussed whether to uptitrate to 600 mg TID to improve symptom control but patient reports the current dose is tolerable and he is concerned about drowsiness. Will hold at current dose. Neurology follow-up appointment in 6 weeks -- will defer to their judgment regarding further pharmacologic management. Occupational therapy has been helpful; patient is using assistive adaptive devices and has modified his home environment (non-slip mats, grab bars in shower). I encouraged him to continue physical therapy exercises for balance. I emphasized that nerve recovery after cumulative oxaliplatin toxicity can continue slowly for 12-18 months but may not fully resolve; realistic expectations discussed.

4. HYPERTENSION -- CONTROLLED ON TRIPLE ANTIHYPERTENSIVE THERAPY:
BP today 136/84 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 10 mg + HCTZ 12.5 mg. This is well-controlled and near goal. Ramucirumab-associated hypertension has been a manageable challenge. No changes to antihypertensives today. Will continue current regimen. Urine protein/creatinine ratio mildly elevated at 0.28 -- below the threshold for ramucirumab dose modification (most guidelines use >2 g/24h as the threshold for hold) but above the lower normal range. Will recheck with next cycle labs. I explained to the patient that the ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) serves a dual purpose: blood pressure control and some renoprotection given the mild proteinuria in the setting of anti-VEGFR therapy.

5. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:
HbA1c today 7.3%, slightly above our target of <7% for this patient. Glucose 122 mg/dL fasting. This mild elevation likely multifactorial: intermittent dexamethasone use as antiemetic during infusions, reduced physical activity due to fatigue and neuropathy, and underlying disease. Continuing metformin 1000 mg BID. Will not add additional antidiabetic agents at this time given the relatively modest elevation and complexity of his overall regimen. Recommended dietary adjustments (reduce refined carbohydrates, increase fiber). Recheck HbA1c in 3 months. Will defer more intensive diabetic management to his primary care physician.

6. ANEMIA:
Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 11.4 g/dL) -- stable compared to prior values. This is consistent with anemia of chronic disease in the setting of metastatic cancer and ongoing chemotherapy. Iron studies were checked last cycle: ferritin was 148 ng/mL (not iron deficient). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not indicated per current oncology guidelines in this context. Will monitor; transfusion threshold would be Hgb <8 g/dL or symptomatic anemia, neither of which applies currently.

7. MAGNESIUM:
Mild hypomagnesemia noted today (1.8 mEq/L). Ramucirumab can contribute to electrolyte disturbances. Starting magnesium oxide 400 mg BID. Dietary counseling given (magnesium-rich foods: leafy greens, nuts, legumes -- the latter of which he tolerates well post-colectomy). Recheck next cycle.

8. QUALITY OF LIFE AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT:
I spent significant time today discussing Mr. Okafor's quality of life and his goals. He is remarkably resilient -- continuing to contribute professionally in a modified capacity, maintaining his home life with his wife, and staying engaged with his care. He expressed some grief about his reduced professional role but said that the work is still meaningful to him. His wife is a significant source of strength and is emotionally well-supported herself per her report. Social work has been engaged previously; I encouraged a refresher visit given the emotional weight of now being in second-line therapy. He is well-informed and directly asked about prognosis; I framed this in terms of continued disease control being our goal, acknowledging that this disease remains metastatic but that he has defied some of the initial predictions and we have further options including clinical trials and HER2-directed approaches should the current regimen stop working.

9. RETURN VISITS AND NEXT STEPS:
- Continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab on current schedule (every 14 days)
- Labs with next infusion (CBC, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9, urine protein/creatinine)
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 8 weeks
- Neurology follow-up in 6 weeks for neuropathy reassessment
- HbA1c recheck in 3 months (coordinate with primary care)
- Magnesium recheck with next cycle labs
- Early phase clinical trials team to contact patient regarding KRAS G12D inhibitor trial eligibility
- Social work follow-up encouraged
- Patient instructed to call the triage nurse line for: diarrhea >4 stools above baseline per day, fever >100.4°F, severe nausea/vomiting, significant change in blood pressure (>160/100 persistently at home), worsening dyspnea, chest pain, or new neurologic symptoms

Time spent: 55 minutes (greater than half of encounter time spent in counseling and coordination of care).

Patient and wife verbalized understanding of the plan. Questions answered. Follow-up scheduled.
