GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY

Pathogenic / Likely Pathogenic Variants — Somatic

1. KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 42%
   Classification: Pathogenic — activating mutation
   Gene Function: KRAS encodes a membrane-associated GTPase critical for RAS/MAPK signal transduction. Codon 12 substitutions lock the protein in a constitutively GTP-bound active state, driving unrestrained downstream ERK/MEK activation.
   Clinical Significance: KRAS codon 12 mutations, including the G12D substitution, are the most prevalent oncogenic alterations in colorectal adenocarcinoma (present in approximately 35-40% of CRC). This variant renders anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) INEFFECTIVE; anti-EGFR agents are contraindicated. The G12D mutation may also have differential sensitivity profiles to investigational KRAS G12D-specific covalent inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133) currently under clinical investigation in CRC. VAF of 42% is consistent with a clonal, founding oncogenic driver in a tumor with estimated 65% cellularity.

2. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 51%
   Classification: Pathogenic — loss-of-function (dominant-negative mechanism)
   Gene Function: TP53 encodes p53, the canonical tumor suppressor governing cell cycle arrest, DNA damage response, and apoptosis. R248W is among the most common TP53 hotspot mutations in human cancer; it both abrogates sequence-specific DNA binding and exerts dominant-negative effects over residual wild-type p53.
   Clinical Significance: Ubiquitous in colorectal cancer (>50% of cases). Contributes to genomic instability and resistance to apoptosis. Not directly actionable with approved therapies; emerging strategies targeting mutant p53 (e.g., APR-246/eprenetapopt) remain investigational. High VAF (51%) confirms a clonal event.

3. APC p.R1450* (c.4348C>T)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48%
   Classification: Pathogenic — truncating loss-of-function (nonsense mutation)
   Gene Function: APC is the gatekeeper tumor suppressor in the canonical WNT/beta-catenin pathway. Biallelic inactivation of APC is the initiating molecular event in the vast majority of sporadic colorectal cancers, leading to nuclear accumulation of beta-catenin and transcriptional activation of oncogenic programs.
   Clinical Significance: The R1450* truncating mutation is a well-characterized somatic APC variant in CRC. VAF of 48% is consistent with biallelic inactivation (second hit via loss of heterozygosity or second somatic mutation). Germline testing (see below) was negative for pathogenic germline APC variants, consistent with sporadic (non-familial adenomatous polyposis) disease.

4. SMAD4 p.R361H (c.1082G>A)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 19%
   Classification: Pathogenic — loss-of-function
   Gene Function: SMAD4 (also known as DPC4) is a central signal transducer in the TGF-beta/BMP tumor suppressor pathway. SMAD4 loss abrogates TGF-beta-mediated growth inhibition and promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition and metastatic dissemination.
   Clinical Significance: SMAD4 inactivation occurs in approximately 25-30% of colorectal carcinomas and is strongly associated with an increased propensity for hepatic metastasis and reduced overall survival. Its presence in this specimen — notably at a lower VAF of 19%, suggesting possible subclonal enrichment in the metastatic deposit — underscores the adverse prognostic implications for this patient. SMAD4 loss has also been associated with reduced benefit from anti-VEGF therapy in some datasets, though this remains exploratory.

5. PIK3CA p.E545K (c.1633G>A)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 18%
   Classification: Pathogenic — activating mutation
   Gene Function: PIK3CA encodes the p110-alpha catalytic subunit of phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase. The E545K substitution in the helical domain constitutively activates PI3K, driving downstream AKT/mTOR signaling and promoting cell survival, proliferation, and metabolic reprogramming.
   Clinical Significance: PIK3CA mutations occur in approximately 15-20% of colorectal cancers, frequently co-occurring with KRAS mutations. In this patient, the concurrent PIK3CA E545K activation and PTEN loss (see below) represent dual activation of the PI3K/AKT pathway. This co-activation has implications for resistance to targeted therapy and may be a future target with PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway inhibitors (investigational in CRC). Subclonal VAF (18%) may reflect intratumoral heterogeneity.

6. FBXW7 p.R465H (c.1394G>A)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 22%
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic — loss-of-function
   Gene Function: FBXW7 encodes an F-box E3 ubiquitin ligase subunit responsible for proteasomal targeting and degradation of key oncoproteins including c-MYC, cyclin E, NOTCH, and JUN. Loss of FBXW7 function leads to accumulation of these pro-oncogenic substrates.
   Clinical Significance: FBXW7 is one of the most commonly mutated tumor suppressors across human cancers, including CRC (approximately 10-15% of cases). The R465H substitution disrupts the WD40 beta-propeller domain, impairing substrate recognition. Its co-occurrence with MYC amplification (see copy number alterations below) suggests convergent mechanisms stabilizing MYC oncogenic activity in this tumor.

Copy Number Alterations — Somatic

7. CDK8 Amplification
   Copy Number: 4 copies (gain above diploid baseline)
   Classification: Pathogenic — copy number gain
   Gene Function: CDK8 is a transcriptional cyclin-dependent kinase functioning as a component of the Mediator complex. CDK8 amplification/overexpression promotes beta-catenin-driven transcription and is a recognized oncogenic event in CRC, particularly in the context of APC-mutant/WNT-activated tumors.
   Clinical Significance: CDK8 amplification occurs in approximately 13% of colorectal carcinomas and is associated with APC mutation and WNT pathway activation. CDK8 kinase inhibitors are under clinical investigation.

8. MYC Amplification
   Copy Number: 6 copies (high-level gain)
   Classification: Pathogenic — oncogenic amplification
   Gene Function: MYC (c-MYC) is a master transcription factor regulating cell growth, proliferation, and metabolism. High-level MYC amplification results in transcriptional addiction and promotes tumor aggressiveness.
   Clinical Significance: MYC amplification is present in approximately 10-15% of metastatic CRCs. In the context of FBXW7 loss-of-function (impaired MYC protein degradation) and CDK8 amplification (enhanced WNT-MYC transcriptional axis), this tumor harbors convergent mechanisms sustaining elevated MYC activity. Associated with aggressive disease biology and potentially shorter time to progression.

9. PTEN Loss — Homozygous Deletion
   Genomic Alteration: Homozygous deletion spanning exons 1-9 (complete gene loss)
   Classification: Pathogenic — complete loss of function
   Gene Function: PTEN is a phosphatase that antagonizes PI3K signaling by converting phosphatidylinositol (3,4,5)-trisphosphate (PIP3) back to PIP2. Homozygous PTEN deletion completely abolishes this brake on AKT/mTOR signaling.
   Clinical Significance: Complete PTEN loss in combination with PIK3CA E545K activating mutation constitutes dual, convergent activation of the PI3K/AKT pathway, a configuration associated with resistance to upstream RAS-targeted agents and potentially enhanced sensitivity to downstream PI3K, AKT, or mTOR inhibitors (investigational in CRC). PTEN loss alone occurs in approximately 30% of metastatic CRCs; dual PTEN loss plus PIK3CA mutation is less frequent and may identify a molecularly distinct subset.

INTERPRETATION AND CLINICAL CONTEXT

This comprehensive genomic profile was performed on a metastatic hepatic deposit of a moderately differentiated sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma in a 58-year-old male with Stage IV, M1a disease (synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases).

The molecular landscape is characteristic of a genomically complex, sporadic MSS colorectal adenocarcinoma. The founding alterations — APC truncating mutation (WNT pathway gatekeeper), TP53 dominant-negative mutation, and KRAS G12D activating mutation — represent the canonical CRC oncogenic triad and are consistent with the chromosomal instability (CIN) molecular subtype, likely corresponding to CMS2 or CMS3 consensus molecular subtype given KRAS mutation and PI3K pathway co-activation.

Key therapeutic implications:
1. ANTI-EGFR THERAPY CONTRAINDICATED: KRAS G12D mutation precludes use of cetuximab or panitumumab in any line of therapy. This is a definitive predictive biomarker of anti-EGFR resistance.
2. IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITION NOT STANDARD: MSS status and low TMB (8 mut/Mb) do not support the use of pembrolizumab or nivolumab as standard therapy.
3. HER2-DIRECTED THERAPY NOT INDICATED: HER2 not amplified in this baseline specimen.
4. PI3K/AKT DUAL ACTIVATION: The co-occurrence of PIK3CA E545K and homozygous PTEN deletion represents a potentially clinically significant finding. This dual activation may confer relative resistance to anti-VEGF therapy and could be a future therapeutic target. Clinical trials investigating AKT inhibitors (e.g., capivasertib, ipatasertib) or PI3K inhibitors in CRC may be relevant for future consideration.
5. SMAD4 LOSS: The SMAD4 R361H loss-of-function variant (VAF 19%, subclonal or enriched in metastatic deposit) is a recognized adverse prognostic marker in CRC, particularly associated with hepatic metastasis propensity and potentially with reduced benefit from anti-VEGF therapy in some retrospective datasets.
6. INVESTIGATIONAL: KRAS G12D-specific covalent inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133 and related compounds) are under active clinical investigation in KRAS G12D-mutant CRC. The patient may be a candidate for clinical trial enrollment in future lines of therapy. Molecular tumor board review of available KRAS G12D-targeted trials is recommended.

No actionable fusions (NTRK, ALK, RET, ROS1, FGFR) were detected. No BRAF V600E mutation is present, precluding use of the encorafenib + cetuximab regimen.

Germline analysis was negative for all hereditary CRC and related syndrome genes tested, confirming sporadic disease.

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION:
This case was presented and discussed at the gastrointestinal multidisciplinary tumor board today, with active participation from medical oncology, hepatobiliary surgery, colorectal surgery, and interventional radiology.

Key genomic and molecular considerations:
- KRAS G12D mutation: This is the dominant driver mutation and, critically, renders the patient ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab). Any RAS-mutant colorectal cancer -- whether KRAS or NRAS -- is a categorical contraindication to EGFR-targeted antibody therapy, and the G12D variant is among the most common and well-characterized.
- MSS/pMMR status: The tumor is microsatellite stable by both IHC and confirmed by NGS. This precludes use of immune checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab (Keytruda), which are approved and effective in MSI-H/dMMR colorectal cancer but offer no benefit in MSS disease.
- SMAD4 p.R361H loss-of-function mutation: This is an important prognostic finding. SMAD4 loss in metastatic colorectal cancer has been associated with worse outcomes, resistance to 5-FU-based regimens, and some data suggest association with bevacizumab resistance -- though bevacizumab remains a reasonable option in the first-line setting.
- PIK3CA E545K activating mutation with concurrent PTEN homozygous deletion: Dual PI3K/AKT pathway activation. This may have biologic implications for resistance to therapy and could be a future therapeutic consideration.
- HER2 status: Not amplified on baseline testing. ERBB2 non-amplified, HER2 IHC not performed at this time given lack of amplification by CGP. However, it is noteworthy that HER2 amplification can emerge as an acquired resistance mechanism in colorectal cancer, particularly under selection pressure from anti-EGFR or anti-VEGF therapy; this will warrant re-evaluation at progression.
- KRAS G12D-specific targeted therapies: Investigational agents such as KRAS G12D-selective inhibitors are in early-phase clinical development. At this time, these agents do not have regulatory approval for colorectal cancer and are not available as standard of care. We will track clinical trial availability.
- Germline testing: Negative for Lynch syndrome and FAP-associated mutations; this is a sporadic colorectal cancer.

Treatment approach decided at tumor board:
Given the bilobar hepatic metastatic burden, the technical challenges to upfront hepatic resection, and the molecular profile (KRAS-mutant, MSS), the tumor board consensus recommendation is initiation of palliative-intent systemic chemotherapy with FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab as first-line therapy. This regimen (oxaliplatin/leucovorin/5-FU with anti-VEGF) is well-established as a first-line backbone in RAS-mutant metastatic colorectal cancer. Anti-EGFR therapy is not indicated given KRAS G12D.

The goals of first-line therapy are dual: (1) systemic disease control and potential tumor downsizing, with the secondary aim of converting hepatic metastases to a resectable or ablatable state (conversion strategy); and (2) palliation of symptoms and maintenance of performance status. If hepatic lesions respond sufficiently after 6-8 cycles, the case will be reassessed at MDT for hepatic-directed therapy -- this could include hepatic resection (if all lesions become amenable to R0 resection with adequate FLR), combination resection and intraoperative ablation, or percutaneous ablation of selected lesions (particularly the segment VI lesion noted by interventional radiology). The sigmoid primary will be addressed on a case-by-case basis; currently, the patient does not have evidence of obstruction and deferral of primary resection to proceed with systemic therapy first is appropriate and supported by published data.

Port placement: Subcutaneous implanted venous access device (port) will be placed prior to chemotherapy initiation given the need for prolonged IV infusional 5-FU access. This will be arranged with general/vascular surgery promptly.

Neuropathy risk: The patient has pre-existing grade 1 bilateral sensory neuropathy from diabetic peripheral neuropathy. This is clinically important as oxaliplatin-induced cumulative peripheral neuropathy is anticipated and may be more problematic given this baseline. He will be monitored closely with low threshold for dose modification.

Blood pressure: Bevacizumab can worsen hypertension. Pre-existing hypertension is already managed with lisinopril. Blood pressure will be closely monitored at each visit; the antihypertensive regimen will be adjusted as needed.

Metformin: May continue metformin; no oxalate crystal formation risk anticipated at standard dosing. Glucose monitoring will be maintained, and we will watch for metformin-associated lactic acidosis with any major surgical procedure or IV contrast administration.

Patient counseling: An extensive discussion was held with Mr. Okafor and his wife regarding the goals of therapy. This is a palliative-intent treatment, as stage IV colorectal cancer is generally not curable with systemic chemotherapy alone. However, a meaningful proportion of patients with liver-limited metastatic colorectal cancer who achieve significant response may be candidates for hepatic-directed therapy, which in select patients can result in prolonged disease control and, rarely, long-term survival. This possibility was presented as a realistic but not guaranteed goal. The expected toxicities of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab were reviewed in detail, including peripheral neuropathy (particularly with his underlying diabetic neuropathy), fatigue, myelosuppression, mucositis, hypertension, potential wound healing issues with bevacizumab, and rare but serious risks including bowel perforation with bevacizumab (low risk given no peritoneal disease), arterial thromboembolic events, and VTE.

Mr. Okafor expressed understanding and consent to proceed. He emphasized his commitment to treatment and desire to remain as functional as possible in his professional role. He was encouraged to inform us of any side effects promptly.

Mr. Okafor was diagnosed approximately 2 months ago with stage IV (T3 N2a M1a) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma following colonoscopy that identified a 4.8 cm sigmoid mass. Workup confirmed synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases involving segments II (1.4 cm), IVa (2.1 cm), VI (1.7 cm), and VII (3.2 cm, dominant lesion). Molecular profiling revealed a KRAS G12D activating mutation, NRAS wild-type, BRAF V600E wild-type, MSS by both IHC and NGS, SMAD4 loss (R361H), PIK3CA E545K, and PTEN homozygous deletion. HER2 non-amplified. TMB low at 8 mut/Mb. Given KRAS mutation status, anti-EGFR agents (cetuximab, panitumumab) are contraindicated. MSS status precludes checkpoint immunotherapy. Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, first-line palliative-intent therapy was initiated with mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus 400 mg/m2 day 1, 5-FU infusion 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg; q14 days). A right-sided subcutaneous port was placed without complication prior to initiation of therapy.

He has now completed three cycles of FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab. Overall, he reports tolerating therapy with moderate but manageable side effects. His primary concerns today are:

1. Peripheral neuropathy: He describes progressive numbness and tingling in bilateral hands and feet that has worsened over the past 3-4 weeks. He notes difficulty with fine motor tasks -- specifically trouble picking up coins, managing buttons on dress shirts, and occasional difficulty holding a pen for writing. The tingling is present at rest and worsens with cold exposure (he has been avoiding touching cold surfaces and drinking cold beverages through a straw). He describes the sensation as "pins and needles" that are constant. He denies frank pain in his hands, but notes some burning discomfort in bilateral feet, particularly in the evenings. He rates the discomfort as a 4/10. He is still walking independently and performing most activities of daily living, but acknowledges these tasks are harder than before. He has been wearing socks to bed, which provides mild relief. It is worth noting that he had pre-existing mild sensory neuropathy (grade 1) in his bilateral feet at baseline, attributed to his longstanding type 2 diabetes, and the current symptoms are a clear progression from that baseline.

2. Elevated blood pressure: He has been monitoring his blood pressure at home and reports consistent readings in the 158-168 mmHg systolic and 96-104 mmHg diastolic range over the last two weeks. Prior to starting chemotherapy, his blood pressure was well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily. He denies headache, visual changes, chest pain, or shortness of breath. He denies any missed doses of his antihypertensive medication.

3. Nausea and fatigue: He reports grade 1 nausea during the 48-72 hours following each infusion, managed effectively with prochlorperazine as needed. He is not requiring scheduled antiemetics between infusions. Fatigue is moderate (grade 2) -- he is continuing to work as a high school principal but leaving earlier in the afternoons and delegating more responsibilities to his vice principals. He is sleeping more than usual.

4. Constitutional: Appetite is decreased but he is maintaining adequate oral intake and his weight has been stable (within 2-3 lbs of baseline). No diarrhea or constipation beyond what was present at baseline. No mouth sores. No skin or hand-foot changes. No alopecia (as expected with this regimen). No fever or chills. No easy bruising or unusual bleeding.

He denies any abdominal pain, change in bowel habits, rectal bleeding, melena, urinary symptoms, neurological symptoms beyond the peripheral neuropathy, or respiratory symptoms. He denies cold-triggered jaw pain (cold-triggered jaw/throat discomfort, the acute oxaliplatin syndrome, was present after cycle 1 but has resolved with his behavioral modifications -- avoiding cold liquids and foods).

Laboratory results reviewed from today's draw:
- CEA: 94.1 ng/mL (down from 187.3 ng/mL at baseline; 49.7% decline)
- CA 19-9: 198 U/mL (down from 412 U/mL at baseline; 51.9% decline)
- CBC: WBC 4.8 x10^9/L, ANC 2,700/mm3 (no neutropenia), Hgb 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, down from 12.9 at baseline; likely chemotherapy-related anemia), Plt 218 x10^9/L
- CMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, Cr 0.94 mg/dL (eGFR >60, stable), LFTs: AST 38 U/L, ALT 42 U/L, Alk Phos 112 U/L, Total Bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL (mild LFT elevation attributed to hepatic metastases, stable or slightly improved from baseline AST 51, ALT 55)
- HbA1c: 7.2% (stable, diabetes well-controlled on metformin)
- Magnesium: 1.7 mEq/L (low-normal; supplementation recommended)
- Albumin: 3.9 g/dL (adequate nutritional status)
- INR: 1.1
- Urinalysis: 1+ protein (new finding; concerning for bevacizumab-related proteinuria; 24-hour urine protein ordered)

The tumor marker trajectory is encouraging and consistent with response to treatment. No clinical signs of bowel obstruction, perforation, or other surgical emergencies. The primary sigmoid tumor has not been resected at this time; the MDT decision at diagnosis was to pursue induction systemic therapy first, with reassessment for hepatic-directed therapy and primary tumor resection after 12 cycles.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive as described in HPI: peripheral sensory neuropathy (bilateral hands and feet, grade 2), fatigue (grade 2), nausea (grade 1, well-controlled), decreased appetite, hypertension (bevacizumab-related, superimposed on pre-existing hypertension), cold sensitivity in hands (acute oxaliplatin syndrome, improving). Mild anemia-related symptoms (increased tiredness).

Negative: No fever, chills, night sweats. No weight loss beyond expected. No chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea. No focal neurological deficits (motor strength intact). No gait instability beyond mild unsteadiness with foot numbness. No visual changes, floaters, or headaches. No abdominal pain. No rectal bleeding, hematochezia, or melena. No hematuria. No rash, hand-foot syndrome, mucositis, or alopecia. No signs of DVT or PE (no leg swelling, no pleuritic chest pain). No signs of wound dehiscence at port site.

- Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (new addition; as of this visit)
- Metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily
- Gabapentin: Not yet started (to be considered if neuropathy worsens)
- Prochlorperazine 10 mg PO q6h PRN nausea
- Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN nausea (infusion day regimen)
- Dexamethasone 12 mg IV premedication on infusion days
- Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily (held given bevacizumab, was part of prior cardiovascular prophylaxis -- discussed holding)
- Magnesium oxide 400 mg PO daily (new; for hypomagnesemia)

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies. No latex allergy. No contrast dye allergy (tolerated IV contrast without premedication at staging imaging).

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (well-controlled, HbA1c 7.2%)
- Hypertension (now bevacizumab-exacerbated, being optimized)

SURGICAL HISTORY

SOCIAL HISTORY
Mr. Okafor is a high school principal, married with three adult children, and lives with his wife. He is of Nigerian-American descent, born in the United States. He quit smoking 12 years ago and has a 15 pack-year history. He consumes alcohol socially, no more than 1-2 drinks per week, and has voluntarily abstained since starting chemotherapy. He has a strong support system and his wife accompanies him to most appointments. He is motivated to continue aggressive treatment and has expressed desire to explore all available options. He has not yet been evaluated for clinical trial eligibility for KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors but has been informed this will be revisited.

FAMILY HISTORY

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: Blood pressure 142/88 mmHg (left arm, seated; repeat 144/90 mmHg), Heart rate 78 bpm, Respiratory rate 16, Temperature 98.4 F, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 88.2 kg (stable), Height 176 cm, BSA 2.01 m2, ECOG Performance Status 1.

General: Alert, oriented, well-nourished, in no acute distress. Appears comfortable at rest. Dressed professionally (suit and tie), consistent with work schedule.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx: mucous membranes moist, no mucositis, no oral ulcerations. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or submandibular chains.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No elevated JVP. Peripheral pulses 2+ in bilateral radial and dorsalis pedis arteries.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender to deep palpation throughout. No palpable hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No palpable abdominal mass. No guarding or rigidity. Bowel sounds normoactive. No ascites by percussion or shifting dullness. The sigmoid mass is not clinically palpable (consistent with deep pelvic location).

Skin: No rash. No hand-foot syndrome (no erythema, blistering, or desquamation of palms or soles). No petechiae or ecchymosis. Port site on right chest wall: well-healed, no erythema, no tenderness, no signs of infection. No flushing.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all four extremities (upper and lower). Reflexes: reduced but present at bilateral Achilles (1+), patellar (2+), biceps (2+). Sensation: reduced light touch and pin-prick in a stocking-glove distribution -- bilateral feet from toes to mid-shin, bilateral hands from fingertips to mid-palm. Vibration sense diminished at bilateral great toes. Two-point discrimination impaired in bilateral index fingers. Romberg testing: mild sway noted but no frank fall (borderline). Gait: slightly cautious, slightly widened base, without frank ataxia. Fine motor testing: difficulty with rapid alternating movements; unable to pick up a paper clip from the table surface but able to pick up a quarter.

Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

LABORATORY AND IMAGING RESULTS
As detailed in HPI above. Key points:
- CEA 94.1 ng/mL (down from 187.3; excellent response trajectory)
- CA 19-9 198 U/mL (down from 412; concordant decline)
- CBC: mild anemia (Hgb 11.4), no neutropenia, platelets adequate
- LFTs mildly elevated but improved from baseline (hepatic metastases effect)
- New finding: 1+ proteinuria on urinalysis -- 24-hour urine protein ordered to rule out bevacizumab-related nephrotic-range proteinuria
- Magnesium 1.7 mEq/L (low-normal; likely oxaliplatin-related renal magnesium wasting)
- Diabetes well-controlled (HbA1c 7.2%)
- No restaging imaging performed at this visit; per protocol, restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis planned after cycle 6

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Raymond Okafor is a 58-year-old male with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, SMAD4-mutant, MSS, M1a hepatic metastases) now completing cycle 3 of first-line mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab. The tumor marker response is encouraging -- CEA has declined approximately 50% and CA 19-9 has declined approximately 52% from baseline, suggesting meaningful biochemical response. His primary treatment-related toxicities are:

1. GRADE 2 PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY (cumulative oxaliplatin toxicity, superimposed on pre-existing diabetic neuropathy):
- Examination confirms stocking-glove distribution of sensory loss with fine motor impairment, consistent with grade 2 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy (OIPN) per CTCAE v5.0 grading (sensory alteration or paresthesia interfering with function but not ADLs).
- Given pre-existing diabetic neuropathy at baseline, the cumulative risk for worsening neuropathy with further oxaliplatin exposure is significant and warrants proactive dose management.
- Plan: Reduce oxaliplatin dose to 75 mg/m2 (approximately 12% reduction from 85 mg/m2) for cycle 4 and subsequent cycles, in accordance with institutional guidelines for grade 2 OIPN.
- The "stop and go" (OPTIMOX-type) strategy -- briefly stopping oxaliplatin and reintroducing later -- was discussed but given the patient's baseline neuropathy risk, we will favor dose reduction and close monitoring with re-escalation contingent on improvement.
- Cold avoidance counseling reinforced (no cold beverages, gloves in cold weather, no cold surfaces).
- If neuropathy worsens to grade 3 (significant functional impairment), oxaliplatin will be discontinued outright.
- Duloxetine 30 mg daily considered for neuropathic symptom relief (discussed ASCO guideline data); however, given his diabetes management and tolerability profile, we will hold for now and reassess at next visit. He is not in significant pain at this time (discomfort 4/10).
- Referral to occupational therapy for adaptive strategies for fine motor limitations placed.
- Neurology consultation to be placed if neuropathy progresses.

2. BEVACIZUMAB-RELATED HYPERTENSION (superimposed on pre-existing hypertension):
- Blood pressure today 142-144/88-90 mmHg despite current lisinopril 10 mg daily.
- Bevacizumab-related hypertension is a well-known and expected on-target toxicity (VEGF inhibition reduces nitric oxide production and increases peripheral vascular resistance). Given his pre-existing hypertension managed at baseline with lisinopril 10 mg, the additional BP burden from bevacizumab has pushed him above goal.
- Plan: Uptitrate lisinopril to 20 mg daily (doubling the dose). Additionally, adding amlodipine 5 mg daily as a second antihypertensive agent (calcium channel blockers are preferred second-line agents in this setting and are well-tolerated with bevacizumab).
- BP goal: less than 140/90 mmHg; target less than 130/80 given his diabetes and ongoing VEGF inhibitor therapy.
- Patient instructed to monitor blood pressure at home twice daily (morning and evening) and call clinic if readings persistently exceed 160/100 mmHg.
- If hypertension becomes refractory or he develops signs of hypertensive urgency/emergency, bevacizumab will be held.
- Reviewed warning signs of bevacizumab toxicity: new-onset severe headache, visual disturbances (RPLS/PRES), rectal/gastrointestinal bleeding, thrombotic events (chest pain, leg swelling, stroke symptoms), poor wound healing.
- Aspirin 81 mg held (it was on his medication list; holding in the setting of bevacizumab to reduce GI bleeding risk).

3. PROTEINURIA (new 1+ on urinalysis):
- Bevacizumab can cause proteinuria through inhibition of VEGF-A in the glomerulus (podocyte injury). Grade 1 proteinuria (urine dipstick 1-2+) is generally permissible to continue bevacizumab; grade 2 (2+ protein consistently or 24-hour urine 2-3.4 g) may warrant dose hold; grade 3 (nephrotic syndrome, 24-hour urine ≥3.5 g) requires discontinuation.
- 24-hour urine protein ordered. If <2 g/24h, bevacizumab can continue without dose modification.
- Renal function (Cr 0.94 mg/dL) is currently stable.
- Follow-up urinalysis with each cycle.

4. MILD ANEMIA (Hgb 11.4 g/dL, grade 1):
- Mild, expected chemotherapy-related anemia. No transfusion indicated at this level.
- No symptoms of significant anemia (no dyspnea, no angina, no dizziness). Fatigue is multifactorial.
- Iron studies, B12, and folate ordered to rule out nutritional deficiency contributing to anemia.
- Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents are not standard in the curative/palliative chemotherapy setting (given bevacizumab and thromboembolic risk considerations).
- Recheck CBC at next visit; if Hgb falls below 8 g/dL, transfusion to be considered.

5. HYPOMAGNESEMIA (Mg 1.7 mEq/L, low-normal):
- Common with oxaliplatin-containing regimens (renal magnesium wasting). Also relevant in the context of diabetes.
- Starting magnesium oxide 400 mg PO daily. Encourage dietary sources of magnesium.
- Recheck magnesium at next visit.

6. TUMOR RESPONSE ASSESSMENT:
- Tumor markers down approximately 50% from baseline after 3 cycles -- consistent with ongoing response. Clinical exam without palpable abdominal mass or signs of obstruction.
- Formal restaging CT scan of chest/abdomen/pelvis will be performed after cycle 6 (approximately 6 weeks from today), per protocol.
- After cycle 6 restaging, will re-present to the multidisciplinary tumor board for re-evaluation of hepatic resection/ablation candidacy.
- Continue mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab at modified doses (oxaliplatin reduced to 75 mg/m2; all other agents at current doses).

7. ONGOING MANAGEMENT:
- Metformin continued for diabetes (eGFR stable, acceptable).
- HbA1c 7.2% -- endocrinology or primary care to continue diabetes management.
- Lipids: continue statin as prescribed by primary care.
- Nutritional support: referral to oncology dietitian placed given decreased appetite; emphasis on adequate protein and caloric intake to maintain performance status.
- Prochlorperazine PRN for nausea: adequate, continue.
- Port care instructions reviewed; site well-healed.

8. GOALS OF THERAPY / COUNSELING:
- Discussed disease status with patient and his wife who is present today. Tumor markers showing encouraging response. Formal imaging assessment after cycle 6 will be the key decision point.
- Patient remains engaged in work life (part-time, less demanding schedule); ECOG PS 1 maintained.
- Acknowledged his concerns about neuropathy impacting his quality of life; reassured him that dose reduction is appropriate and will be protective; emphasized that stopping oxaliplatin entirely remains an option if needed and does not end treatment.
- KRAS G12D clinical trial eligibility: patient remains in tumor registry. Our early-phase trials team is monitoring for appropriate trials (KRAS G12D-selective inhibitors in CRC). Will revisit after restaging imaging.

RETURN TO CLINIC: 4 weeks (cycle 5 of FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab; also to review 24-hour urine protein result, blood pressure response to medication change, and neuropathy trajectory). Cycle 4 infusion scheduled in the infusion center as usual.
